perturbation.original_text: For the purpose of this Agreement, “Confidential Information” means any information received by a Party (the “Receiving Party”) regarding the other Party’s (the “Disclosing Party”) business, research, products and/or services, such as all information and technology, including without limitation, research, inventions, manufacture methods, data, designs, plans, drawings, know-how, IT systems, software, processes, schematics, blueprints, records, reports, models, prototypes and descriptions related thereto, customers, partners, as well as the terms and conditions of this Agreement and information furnished during discussions or oral presentations, whether or not designated as confidential at the time of disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: For the purpose of this Agreement, “Confidential Information” means any information received by a Party (the “Receiving Party”) regarding the other Party’s (the “Disclosing Party”) business, research, products and/or services, such as all information and technology, including without limitation, research, inventions, manufacture methods, data, designs, plans, drawings, know-how, IT systems, software, processes, schematics, blueprints, records, reports, models, prototypes and descriptions related thereto, customers, partners, as well as the terms and conditions of this Agreement and information furnished during discussions or oral presentations, only if designated as confidential at the time of disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: The modification adds a critical condition: information is confidential ONLY if explicitly labeled as such during disclosure. This contradicts the original definition, which broadly includes information *whether or not* designated as confidential. This introduces an in-text contradiction because it narrows the scope of what's protected, impacting later sections that assume a broader definition. For instance, accidental disclosures of sensitive information not explicitly marked confidential would now be exempt from protection, undermining the NDA's purpose. This creates uncertainty in enforcement regarding what information is actually protected under the agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Confidentiality and Non-Use 2.1 For the purpose of this Agreement, “Confidential Information” means any information received by a Party (the “Receiving Party”) regarding the other Party’s (the “Disclosing Party”) business, research, products and/or services, such as all information and technology, including without limitation, research, inventions, manufacture methods, data, designs, plans, drawings, know-how, IT systems, software, processes, schematics, blueprints, records, reports, models, prototypes and descriptions related thereto, customers, partners, as well as the terms and conditions of this Agreement and information furnished during discussions or oral presentations, only if designated as confidential at the time of disclosure. 2.2 Confidential Information shall not include information that: i. is or becomes part of the public domain through no fault or breach on the part of Receiving Party; ii. was previously known to the Receiving Party free of any obligation to keep it confidential, as evidenced by its business records; iii. is lawfully obtained by the Receiving Party from a third party; or iv. is independently developed by the Receiving Party without the use of any Confidential Information, as evidenced by its records.
perturbation.justification: The changed text adds the condition 'only if designated as confidential at the time of disclosure'. This directly contradicts the original text's implicit allowance for information to be considered confidential regardless of whether it was explicitly designated as such at the time of disclosure. Since the contradicted text repeats the changed text's language which is contradictory to the original, there is an in-text contradiction. This change narrows the scope of 'Confidential Information', leading to inconsistencies in the interpretation and enforcement of confidentiality obligations throughout the agreement, which qualifies as an inconsistency-based in-text contradiction as per the provided definition.

perturbation.original_text: All Confidential Information (including but not limited to all intellectual property rights emanating from or related to Confidential Information) remains the exclusive property of the Disclosing Party and nothing in this Agreement grants any right or license in the Confidential Information to the Receiving Party.
perturbation.changed_text: All Confidential Information (including but not limited to all intellectual property rights emanating from or related to Confidential Information) becomes the shared property of both Parties. This Agreement grants a perpetual, irrevocable license in the Confidential Information to the Receiving Party.
perturbation.explanation: This change reverses the ownership and licensing terms. Originally, the Disclosing Party retained exclusive ownership, and no license was granted. The modification makes the information shared property and grants a perpetual, irrevocable license to the Receiving Party. This is a direct in-text contradiction. The change directly undermines the provisions outlined to ensure confidentiality and control of intellectual property, and creates a significant contradiction in enforcing the agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Intellectual Property Rights 4.1 All Confidential Information (including but not limited to all intellectual property rights emanating from or related to Confidential Information) becomes the shared property of both Parties. This Agreement grants a perpetual, irrevocable license in the Confidential Information to the Receiving Party.
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the concept of retained exclusive ownership by the Disclosing Party, stating instead that the Confidential Information becomes shared property and granting a perpetual, irrevocable license to the Receiving Party. This directly conflicts with the contradicted text, which states the same change about the Confidential Information becoming shared property and granting a perpetual license, solidifying the contradiction. The explanation confirms that the modification makes the information shared property and grants a perpetual, irrevocable license to the Receiving Party, which is a direct in-text contradiction. This satisfies the definition of 'Inconsistencies - In Text Contradiction' because the changed section directly conflicts with the contradicted text, creating an in-text contradiction regarding ownership and licensing.

perturbation.original_text: Notwithstanding the right to receive liquidated damages, the Disclosing Party has the right to take any other legal measures available and to claim and receive compensation for damages exceeding any liquidated damages, including but not limited to loss of profit.
perturbation.changed_text: The Disclosing Party's sole and exclusive remedy for any breach of this Agreement shall be the liquidated damages specified above. The Disclosing Party waives the right to take any other legal measures available and to claim and receive compensation for damages exceeding any liquidated damages, including but not limited to loss of profit.
perturbation.explanation: This modification creates an in-text contradiction by altering the remedies available to the Disclosing Party in case of a breach. The original clause preserves the right to pursue legal measures beyond liquidated damages. The changed clause limits the remedy exclusively to liquidated damages, waiving all other rights. This is directly contradictory and creates uncertainty in enforcement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Liquidated Damages [only applicable for agreements between commercial partners] 5.1 In the case of any breach of any material obligations under this Agreement by the Receiving Party or any permitted third party who has received Confidential Information according to this Agreement, the Receiving Party shall be liable to pay liquidated damages to the Disclosing Party amounting to SEK [amount] for each such breach of its obligations. The Disclosing Party's sole and exclusive remedy for any breach of this Agreement shall be the liquidated damages specified above. The Disclosing Party waives the right to take any other legal measures available and to claim and receive compensation for damages exceeding any liquidated damages, including but not limited to loss of profit.
perturbation.justification: The changed text explicitly limits the Disclosing Party's remedy to liquidated damages, waiving the right to pursue other legal measures or claim compensation for damages exceeding the liquidated amount. The contradicted text, which is in the same location, uses the exact same language which directly clashes with the original language. The original text preserves the right to pursue other legal measures beyond liquidated damages. This direct conflict creates a strong in-text contradiction because the Disclosing Party cannot simultaneously have and waive the right to pursue remedies beyond liquidated damages. This satisfies the 'Inconsistencies - In Text Contradiction' definition.
